stimulus: More than fifteen years ago, in a noted experiment in the economics of decision-making, professor Dilip Soman placed a small café cart at the edge of a hospital cafeteria. The number of pastry varieties offered to passing ______blank some customers were shown eighteen distinct items, others only four. Strikingly, the customers facing fewer options spent more time at the cart and made larger purchases.
stem: Which choice completes the text so that it conforms to the conventions of Standard English?
rationale:
Choice D is the best answer. The convention being tested is the use of a colon within a sentence. The colon is used in a conventional way to introduce the following description of how the number of pastry varieties offered fluctuated.

Choice A is incorrect because using "while" to join the main clause ("The number...fluctuated") with what follows wrongly suggests that the fluctuation is in contrast to certain customers being shown more options. Choice B is incorrect because "while" suggests a simultaneous occurrence that the sentence does not intend. Choice C is incorrect because a comma alone produces a comma splice between two main clauses.
